title: Goal-Oriented Lower-Tail Calibration of Gaussian Processes for Bayesian Optimization
abstract:
Bayesian optimization (BO) selects evaluation points for expensive
black-box objectives using Gaussian process (GP) predictive
distributions.
Kernel choice and hyperparameter selection can lead to miscalibrated
predictive distributions and an inappropriate exploration--exploitation trade-off. 
For minimization, sampling criteria such as expected improvement (EI) depend on
the predictive distribution below the current best value, so lower-tail
miscalibration directly affects the sampling decision.
This article studies goal-oriented calibration of GP predictive distributions
below a low threshold $t$ in the noiseless setting, for standard GP models with
hyperparameters selected by maximum likelihood. 
A framework for predictive reliability below $t$ is introduced, based on two
notions of spatial calibration: occurrence calibration over the design
space and thresholded $\mu$-calibration on sublevel sets of the form $\lbrace x\in\mathbb{X}, f(x)\le t \rbrace$. 
Building on this framework, we propose tcGP, a post-hoc method that calibrates
GP predictive distributions below $t$, and we show that the resulting EI-based
global optimization algorithm remains dense in the design space. 
Experiments on standard benchmarks show improved lower-tail calibration and BO
performance relative to standard GP models and globally calibrated GP models.
decision_comment: This paper studies an important question in Bayesian optimization. The reviewers generally agreed that the paper is technically solid and that the main idea is interesting and useful. The main concerns were about scope: the work is limited to the noiseless setting, the experiments are mostly on low-dimensional synthetic benchmarks, and some stronger baselines and clearer presentation would help. The rebuttal addressed many of these points and clarified the intended scope, although some concerns about broader practical relevance remained. Overall, I think the paper makes a meaningful contribution within a narrow but valid setting, and I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: Paper derives approaches to Gaussian process (GP) -based Bayesian optimization to address the challenges of miscalibrated predictive uncertainty in the lower tail of the distribution for expected improvement -based active sampling. Following the recent work on probabilistic tail calibration and relaxed GP (ReGP), a spatial below threshold calibration (tcGP) is proposed. tcGP is theoretically derived and analysed and empirically evaluated against different variants of the proposed technique as well as related methods from the literature on deterministic test functions optimisation with some performance improvement against vanilla GP and computational efficiency against reGP.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Paper considers important problem in Bayesian optimization. It is well-motivated and illustrated at the beginning. Theoretical background and guarantees are derived to support the methodological choices. The presentation of spatial and goal-oriented calibration (sections 4 and 5) are a bit hard to follow, and would be good to support with some illustrations and summarise with an algorithm description. Empirical evaluations shown the usefulness and computational efficiency of the proposed approach. Form experimental perspectives, only deterministic test function are considered; the use of more realistic noisy real-world examples could improve the significance and  soundness of the proposed methods.

Strengths
- Well-motivated and illustrated challenge  
- Theoretical guarantees and backup for proposed approach
- Improvements against vanilla GP and computational efficiency against reGP

Weakness 
- Only deterministic functions examined
- Presentation of the method could be more clearly structured (sections 4 and 5)
- Paper misses general discussion and conclusion section
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: - How would proposed approach handle more realistic noisy functions in the optimisation?

reviews.summary: The authors introduce a new framework for calibrating the predictive distribution of a Gaussian process used in a Bayesian optimization (BO) setting, focusing on the tails of the distirbution below a threshold. Departing from the standard Gaussian predictive distribution, they model it instead with a parameterized generalized normal distribution. The parameters are estimated in a goal-oriented way with measures of calibration, which are estimated with leave-one-out and reweighted samples. Two such measures of calibration are introduced: the thresholded one and the occurence one. For incorporation inside the BO loop, they focus on the expected improvement criterion and derive a closed-form formula for the generalized normal distribution. They evaluate their approach against several competitors on 4 analytical test cases (in the main paper, 5 additional ones in the supplementary).
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The framework introduced in this paper is a smart combination of previous ideas originating from (a) the relaxed GP of Petit et al. (for the focus on lower tails part of the predictive distribution in BO) and (b) the calibration of the generalized normal family of Pion and Vasquez. Such combination is however not straightforward, and the authors make a veyr good job at justifying and deriving all the necessary steps: definition of what calibration below a threshold means, with the introduction of two different notions, careful and sound estimation of these calibration measures with leave-one-out and reweighted sampling, and new analytical EI criterion computed for their predictive distribution. All in one, this paper is correct and rigorous, with a pedagogical writing which is easy to follow. In addition, the ideas introduced here are quite original, with a good impact in BO since it can be plugged on top of any GP-based method since it only uses a predictive mean and a predictive variance.

In my opinion, there are only two main weaknesses in the paper. First, the advantage over relaxed GPs appears to be moderate in the numerical experiments. The good news is that it shows that focusing on the lower tails of the predictive distribution is highly beneficial in BO, but may tend me to think that the reweighted leave-one-out machinery is not an essential addition. I think a way to mitigate this weakness would be to insist on the fact that, contrary to relaxed GPs, the calibration procedure introduced here is general and can be applies broadly (as I point out above). Second, the numerical experiments in the main paper only consist of 4 low-dimensional test cases, and I think this section should be reinforced with additional ones, either in higher dimension or a real-world example. There are more results in the Appendix, but the authors do not reference them in the main paper. In addition, the conclusions in the Appendix are rather scattered and it is hard to get an honest and summarized picture of the best performing methods, while some test cases are mentioned in Table 1 but I was unable to find the associated results (Branin and Beale).
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Thanks to the clarity of the presentation, I think I understood the algorithm well, but I still have questions mainly related to the estimation of the different quantities involved in the procedure:
- What is the computational cost of minimizing $J(\beta,\lambda)$? Each call to the objective function involves leave-one-out, and an optimization with an initial large grid for $(\beta,\lambda)$ and then a local optimization algorithm, and several $u$ values to test. This may appear as a very expensive machinery
- The weights are estimated with KDE, and in high dimensions with low sample sizes (due to the threshold), this may be rather imprecise. In addition, why choose the very naive Scott's rule-of-thumb?
- Combining the estimation errors of  $J(\beta,\lambda)$ and KDE, I think that the estimated $(\beta,\lambda)$ may be sub-optimal. Would there be a way to account for its uncertainty when computing the acquisition function? Is there a hope to get a more robust estimate?
- Related to the computational cost, it is mentioned that relaxed GPs are more demanding. Is this statement accounts for the optimization of $J(\beta,\lambda)$?
reviews.limitations: I was unable to find a clear statement in the paper discussing the limitations of the introduced method. Maybe these limitations are scarce, but some parts of the algorithm can appear to have some (see my questions above) and should at least be commented.

reviews.summary: This paper studies calibration of Gaussian process predictive distributions in Bayesian optimization. It proposes a goal-oriented calibration method that focuses on the lower tail of the predictive distribution. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks show improved optimization performance compared with standard GP models.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
# Strengths
The paper provides a clear motivation by showing that miscalibration in the lower tail of GP predictive distributions can negatively affect improvement-based acquisition functions in Bayesian optimization.
# Weaknesses
1.	The proposed method encourages more exploration, which may not be beneficial when the evaluation budget is limited or problems are high-dimensional.
2.	The experiments are conducted on only several low-dimensional synthetic benchmark functions.
3.	The paper reports optimization performance using excursion probability. However, readers may also be interested in seeing the objective values or regret metrics.
4.	The paper does not include a conclusion section.
5.	Typo: Line 161 contains an additional full stop.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1.	How sensitive is the proposed method to the choice of the threshold parameter?
2.	If the Gaussian process model is accurate, could the proposed method lead to unnecessary exploration and thus consume more evaluations?
3.	Would the proposed calibration method remain effective in high-dimensional problems or noisy cases?
reviews.limitations: The paper discusses the potential societal impact, but it does not explicitly discuss the limitations of the proposed method.

reviews.summary: The paper focuses on improving Bayesian optimization by calibrating Gaussian process predictive distributions specifically in their lower tails (for minimization problems), rather than globally. The work aims to address the concept of goal-oriented calibration: since acquisition functions like expected improvement (EI) depend primarily on the predictive mass below the current best value, miscalibration in the lower tail can distort exploration-exploitation trade-offs even when overall calibration is reasonable. The authors introduce two notions of spatial calibration below a threshold t — occurrence calibration and thresholded µ-calibration — and propose tcGP, a post-hoc method that models standardized GP residuals with a generalized normal (GN) family, selecting shape and scale parameters to jointly optimize both calibration notions. A closed-form EI expression under the GN model is derived, a convergence (denseness) result is established, and experiments on standard benchmarks are reported.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths
* The paper identifies an important issue in Bayesian optimization: acquisition functions depend disproportionately on predictive lower tails, meaning global calibration metrics may not align with BO performance.
* The decomposition of tail calibration into occurence and thresholded mu-calibration is neat and conceptually useful
* The ablation study (Section 6.2) comparing tcGP-thres, tcGP-occ, and the joint tcGP is particularly informative 
* The calibration framework is carefully formalized, and mathematically rigorous.
* Convergence guarantee: while the result is primarily a sanity check (denseness under fixed hyperparameters, it is nontrivial because the GN parameters (β_n, λ_n) change at each iteration, and the proof correctly handles this via compactness arguments. 
* Empirical results validate that lower-tail calibration improves optimization performance.

## Weaknesses
* The paper only considers the noiseless setting. This greatly restricts the applicability of the method and the utility of the method for practical problems, which are frequently noisy.
* Evaluation only contains relatively low-d problems.
* The notation is pretty cumbersome. I suggest simplifying where possible (e.g. a superscript binary `j` to distinguish between calibrated/uncalibrated)
* No evaluation of the sensitivity of the method to the weighted LOO. Do the weights remain and accurate on high-dimensional problems? Is stability an issue?
* The choice of delta matters substantially (Figures 6-7), and different values are optimal for different methods (delta=0.05 for tcGP, delta=0.25 for reGP). The paper uses fixed δ values across all problems. An adaptive rule for δ would strengthen the method, and its absence is a practical limitation.
* Benchmark functions are low-dimensional and synthetic. How does the method perform on higher dimensional problems?
* The authors calibrate at t_n , which is above m_n, because calibration at m_n is infeasible (no observations below m_n). This is a fundamental limitation: the calibration target and the level that matters for EI are different. How much does this discrepancy affect performance (if you where able to calibrate at m_n in some omniscient setting).
* It seems like comparing to other BO approaches such as Thompson sampling variants, robust BO methods, or model-misspecification techniques
* Lack of real world problems raises the question of the practical utility of the work
* The convergence result uses fixed hyperparameters in whereas the adaptive hyperparameters are used in practice, so there is a disconnect between the theory and the actual method.
* Missing some baselines for BO with model misspecification (Misspecified Gaussian Process Bandit Optimization, Bogunovic et al, 2021) and fully bayesian methods that integrate over the GP hyperparameter posterior (Practical Bayesian Optimization of Machine Learning Algorithms, Snoek et al, 2012).
* The best method varies by problem.
The scope of the work is limited to the single objective case, but many practical use cases have multiple outcomes (objectives and or constraints).
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
* "In higher dimensions, similar behavior can arise when the selected hyperparameters lead to local overconfidence in sparsely sampled regions, with underestimated lower-tail probabilities and reduced exploration. " Isn't limiting exploration often desirable in finite budget regimes where over-exploration can lead to worse performance (e.g., Hvarfner et al, 2024)? How well does tcGP work on higher-dimensional BO problems?
* How can the calibration methods in the paper be extended to noisy settings?
* Is there any theoretical relationship between tail calibration error and EI regret?
* It would be cool to extend this work to more practical use cases. Constrained single objective optimization seems pretty straightforward where t_n is slightly worse than the constraint bound. What about multi-objective optimization? Batch optimization seems feasible if one used sequential greedy point selection
